speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, [CLEARS THROAT] we last, uh, spoke about your being in Majdanek. What was the kind of work you had to do when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, [CLEARS THROAT] in Majdanek, we-- we [INAUDIBLE] the continuation, make [CLEARS THROAT] basket grenades, with-- with the same group of people what we've been trained, in a way, trained. It's not the big tour or a big spiel. We done it-- the same thing over there-- over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a basket that held a lot of grenades or an outer casing for one grenade? I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Thousands of basket-- thousands and thousands of basket.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big is a basket that holds grenades?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a big grenade, a big-- like a missile. It's gren-- it's basket to hold a missile. And we make--

speaker: interviewer
text: These were hand grenades that you put in those?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not. I worked in-- in-- in-- in, uh, in Berlitz. I worked by hand grenades. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is something different?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something different. This is just the basket to a grenade. But you put the grenade, you stock the grenade, or you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it l-- for a bomb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say small bomb, [INAUDIBLE] a small missile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it go in an airplane? Would be-- sent from an airplane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like you see in an airplane. Uh, uh, a basket was so long, and about-- I would say two feet by-- by forty [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know. Uh, like you make basket, a regular basket, like the [POLISH], you call this in Polish. Um--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean it was woven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Woven, yeah. That's the [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: From grass, from dried grasses. W-- from, um--

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]. I don't know how to say in English. You know like a regular basket?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing. [INAUDIBLE] was more [? green, ?] or more-- yeah. And what did you do? What was your part of the work? Uh, [LAUGH], I got the good part to help my wife. I-- I take away the-- [CLEARS THROAT] let's say, uh, the woman [? got ?] the commander SS lady brought every day the woman. But I would say about 100, 120 woman, the woman been under a different sv-- field. She brought them in the morning to this factory where we make-- what I done, I check the basket if there was good made. And I stock them until, um, the guys came and take it away. Then we-- l-- helped him to load them. But I checked how many. Give the woman specialty done repair sh-- [INAUDIBLE] that brought the baskets from many different states and different places to repair them. The woman repaired-- let's say each took, uh, so-- so much time to repair, eight or 10. Uh, my wife, I took away 10 good one and then gave her 10 good one. You know how to survive. You try to do. And I [? accept ?] my wife [? Edith ?] to help me in [INAUDIBLE]. When they come, instead they give you like pens, and jacket, the stripes. One was for a guy. Let's say he's 7 feet tall, and, uh, so many foot wide. They give it to you. They didn't give you-- they give you one shoe, a big one, and one. And this time, my Edith came to help. I give her the-- uh, the suit. She took it to her barrack. And she know how to sew. She made it a beautiful suit, like, like [INAUDIBLE] the factor or [INAUDIBLE] from the tailor. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This-- over there, was a lady, a SS lady. When she brought the woman, the girls, to work, in this field, to the oil field. And she saw me when I was wearing this suit. And I always tried. This was very important. Your appearance was very important. I always, uh, tried to-- to give away a part in your meal or whatever to have a blade to be shaved, and things like this. And she saw me. She called me into the office. And she took me [? for ?] whole people, or whole [INAUDIBLE]. I-- it's what was my help. She was, uh, a sexy woman, a fat woman. And she have, uh, contacted the couples, and the German couples. They were from [INAUDIBLE]. They were banded [INAUDIBLE]. And she have [INAUDIBLE]. She have contact with him. She was afraid she could get--

speaker: interviewer
text: She slept with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she sleep with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I didn't-- [INAUDIBLE] she must sleep with him, positively. I watched always where she go, where the couple go, where the other guy-- anything which she have to do with. I've been [INAUDIBLE]. She even, later on, asked me to-- this was a new commander. She asked me to bring, uh, a girl was able to cook. And I took a girl from the-- from, uh, the people. It was so happened she was a [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know if she's alive or not. And she cooked. She cooked. I, in Majdanek, uh, I eat. What she eat, I eat. If she drink whiskey, she give me. She likes me in a way. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She took me [INAUDIBLE] I think because I was neatly dressed. I was clean. And this time, new transport, they come before they organize. Everybody tried to organize, change their own shoes. They all got long one. And everybody tried to do and to make it presentable. Is-- I think this was the reason why she liked me. She picked-- she need a guy like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the gopher?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I brought the [INAUDIBLE]. I brought the bowls and things like that. I done everything [INAUDIBLE]. Anything what she need, I done for her. Cleaning shoes-- I done everything., what she-- what she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything that you were worried about? Did you do anything that embarrassed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. She was very-- uh, she-- one day, she watched me too. Because I done lot-- a lot of thing outside the office in a-- in a camp like this. There was 30, 40,000 people. It was a big appelplatz. Appelplatz means where you hold concentrated people. She watched me one time. Uh, uh, uh, they called her [NON-ENGLISH]. An SS man, he saw me. I do not productive work. I'm doing just [INAUDIBLE] and around doing. I also done with the baskets. So anything I finished the work taking back the basket, and sort into good-- something like this. You've got a pretty good idea. He watched me. And he caught me. He picked me up. I w-- he would finish [INAUDIBLE]. And she saw. She took me out. And she talked him out to me. He let me go. I would be dead. Just a complaint in, uh, from a German, an SS man, you are a dead [INAUDIBLE]-- you were a dead pigeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in my Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months. We'd been in Majdanek. We came around, uh-- we came around in-- in-- I think in March, in February or March. And we left-- we left Majdanek, must be June, July, August. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] we went to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same with transport. [INAUDIBLE] you know what she do, [POLISH]. We called-- I called her [POLISH]. [POLISH]. That's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the SS lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS lady. I called her mother, [POLISH]. She even-- uh, it's something like this. You know, [INAUDIBLE] is dead or alive. She-- and I told her. We go in and [INAUDIBLE] many different ways. We [INAUDIBLE] us. We gone in transport. As I say, transport means dead. If you haven't got food, you will get in the [? wagon ?] with 30, 40 people, you haven't got accumulate. She-- uh, my field was connected. Our field was, let's say, um, at least a half a mile big-- a-- a field with thousands of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: She took food, accumulate food. She threw it over, food, to eat it. You know what that's mean? It's all in a million. No. The SS man threw it over-- food for my wife. She know my wife, because she works in the [INAUDIBLE]. She lead the [INAUDIBLE], the basket factory. Did Edith come to the next place with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went until Brunnlitz. Until, uh, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: From over there, we went to Plaszow. And Plaszow--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow was a regular concentration. It was-- I would say it was, uh, between 20,000 and 30,000 [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, our transport take-- took [INAUDIBLE] two days, something. [INAUDIBLE] how they the locomotion or how they got the [INAUDIBLE] or whatever they got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter or summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when we left Majdanek. We came to Majdanek, it was winter. We went to Majdanek--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said June, you thought, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was June, July, Augo--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been over there a few months, quite a few months, until the end of the year, we've been over there in-- in-- in Plaszow. How we went from-- from Plaszow, everything went so fast. I cannot give you exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what did you do in Plaszow? Tell me what you did there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, I done lot of things, anything. Uh, I done-- what they called, be electrician. I [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. It's OK. That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been an electrician. What do you think electrician? To put up bulbs or to put up-- make a connection. If it-- and a lot of things you learn how to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like in Plaszow? How did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, it was very-- it was very bad. Here [? it ?] comes again, Edith. If you know how to organize, um, it was very, very bad. It's-- later, when I got the [? job ?] in Plaszow, by, uh, by uh, Goeth. The guy-- the bad guy from the-- I took care of his dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amon Goeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I took care of the dog, I went [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] I went to work for him is because you take care. You think. In your mind, dogs is-- is meat. If it's meat, it's food. You help yourself. You have-- [INAUDIBLE]. Over there, later on I was glad I got [INAUDIBLE], because you were always was in the [INAUDIBLE] this guy, Goeth. He comes not very often, to play with the dogs, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you take care of the dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a special kennel for dogs? How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he got the nice home. He got the home and beautiful villa, not in camp. Outside the camp. They got-- they got horses. He got a horse. Uh, I took care of horses in Radom. I took-- there's a whole story about the horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had experience taking care of horses before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned. I learned to. I got guys where they know how to handle horses. And I took care once of a horse, and the lagerfuhrer, a German-- but that, oh, son of a bitch. And-- and-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. No problem. You can say anything you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: This guy was-- I learned with-- with guys have been in a town in Poland, where they got horses. And I learned. As I took care of this horse, I learned in a short time, in a week or two. He wouldn't believe. He told the guy where I took-- I forget his name-- [INAUDIBLE]. He liked so much because I took good care of this horse. And-- and this was in Plaszow. Uh, he-- no. This was is Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now here you are in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now in Plaszow--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you volunteered to take care of the dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: The dogs. Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many dogs did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four German shepherds. Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know-- how did you know how to take care of dogs? Did you have to train therm or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you ask me a [INAUDIBLE] a question. If you have to do, you do, and you don't ask how you do, and when you do. I don't know myself I done. I done it to stay alive. I done it-- naturally, you start to feed that dog. If you give him food, he's your friend. You his friend. And I got meat. He got meat. Later on--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- and where did you get the food to feed the dog?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give you-- they told you a place where to go, where to get the food. For the Germans, they have been very, very organized, even in camps, specialty German-- very, very organized in everything what they're doing. Very [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you take care of these dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months, a couple months. I accidentally-- uh, I went away in this in-- in-- in the-- in camp. A Jewish, uh, like a couple, [INAUDIBLE] couple. I've been with them [? in ?] [? Schindler. ?] He caught-- he need work. This is the reason I got to-- to-- to Schindler. They called me to Wieliczka. Oh, and they need some workers in Wieliczka. Wieliczka been, uh-- a [NON-ENGLISH], [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what's a [NON-ENGLISH]. Um, uh, hell. You go to-- if you die, you go to hell. This [INAUDIBLE] a [NON-ENGLISH]. Jewish people call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. You-- you're going to hell. The hell was-- [LAUGH] was not so much the hard work [INAUDIBLE] [? ration. ?] Lice. Like this, you couldn't be 15, 20 minutes that you have to go take it off and make yourself. If not, you couldn't exist. You got the flesh was sore. That's when Schindler came in. And when he brought that from over there to-- to-- to Brunnlitz, it's the first thing what he done. He done, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Disinfectant?

speaker: interviewee
text: A disinfectant place. You bless him for [? this. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, here you are now. Now you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. They caught you. They-- they took you to-- wa-- how do you pronounce? The salt mines?

speaker: interviewee
text: The salt mine. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And salt mine, we got back to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work in the salt mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. I don't remember. Maybe a month, two, six week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What was it like in the salt mine? Tell us what the work was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: The work-- [INAUDIBLE] to-- to-- to get the [INAUDIBLE] the ground, like the-- you-- like coal, like any mines, like-- like the [INAUDIBLE] get gold. The same way [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to dig.

speaker: interviewee
text: They dig. Yeah. And from over there, we went back to-- and thank god we [INAUDIBLE] over there. We went back to-- before the-- the-- the, uh, the Russian approach that transported closer to Germany. The-- [INAUDIBLE]. We went to Plaszow, back. And in Plaszow, they make transport [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go away to Germany already. This is the reason brought me to-- to-- Schindler. I haven't been with Schindler. They got Jews in Krakow. He got them-- it's a whole story for themselves you heard already. Is because the-- the guy, the lagerfuhrer, the German-- you take care of my Jews and I take care of you Jews. He put all of them together. It was a group, whatever, by 800 maybe left. And he connect our names [INAUDIBLE] Wieliczka. [INAUDIBLE] was, um, PWs from Poland-- strong guys. He connected us with the-- with the Krakow Jews. They make one transport. And-- and-- and this is the reason I-- I-- I was on the list from Schindler. And we then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get on the list. Who put your name on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I'm telling you. You have to figure out a little bit by yourself. We-- we-- let's see. I, myself as a group, just a few guys, came to the people where they've been in Wieliczka. We've been there maybe a week, two. I don't remember. He-- then Schindler organized Brunnlitz or [? Bernanzio. ?] He took the-- the-- the Jews from Wieliczka, connected us with Schindler's Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ones from Emalia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it was in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we become [? one ?] transport. The Wieliczka Jews, this is the reason I came over here. If not, I don't know. Maybe I would be-- my wife haven't been with Schindler. And she survived. I would maybe survive without Schindler. I-- I could survive everywhere. [LAUGH] They had put us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was together, but fell from the 13,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us from over there, from Plaszow. Like I said before, the Russian come close to Poland. They transported us to-- to-- to Germany. Is, um-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the reason what Schindler comes in. He done good, that way, I think. He watched it fell from the 1,300 to 1,100. He watched them like a hawk. He couldn't bring us direct to-- to-- to this town where he developed the factory. He took us to another concentration camp-- that camp, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Called, um-- oh, God. Um, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Breninski. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Breninski. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [? Breninski ?] was-- Gross-Rosen. Gross-Rosen. Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like at Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like Majdanek. What they done, the first day we came, they-- they [INAUDIBLE]. They take everything away from you. And you go in, uh, put you naked. This was around-- must be around January. It was so cold, and you're naked. In-- it was not ourselves. It was a group from other camps. The transport they took together, is so many locomotion they got [INAUDIBLE]. It's-- it's-- I'm never going to forget this. Is, you know like a circus, the part [INAUDIBLE] the whole in the circus, an empty circus? They ran the people. And-- and after you shower, they ran us under the circus. The transport, maybe 2,000, maybe 3,000, maybe 4,000. Is the people were there. It's cold inside. [INAUDIBLE] inside. Everybody's cold and pushed themself the inside. The part of the inside was almost-- after they finished is dead, because they killed themself. You know, the-- if you feel cold, you try to get [INAUDIBLE] to the other body. The outside was frozen. Let's say in this time let's say it was maybe two-- I-- I'm not exaggerating. Maybe 2,000, 3,000. It's, uh,  maybe a couple--

speaker: interviewer
text: Crammed into one space.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: People smashed into a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Smashed into the other, because in the outside, [? they pushed in. ?] It's cold. Like a fire, [INAUDIBLE] get out the fire. [INAUDIBLE] from over there-- this was in Gross-Rosen. How they got from over there, the same group, what he got in [INAUDIBLE] in Krakow. He make up the Schindler's list. He got us to Gross-Rosen. From Gross-Rosen, he guarded us. He keep us all of them together-- the same whole group. This is the reason we came alive and we finished alive. The-- this was the-- the [? event ?] of Schindler. I can finish up here, [INAUDIBLE], not to bring you--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Just tell me now. Gro-- you're in Gross-Rosen. You're all jammed together. They disinfected you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross-Rosen, they called us in German, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]-- how are you going to say [GERMAN]? Uh, temporarily you-- you are going in transport temporarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in transit.

speaker: interviewee
text: In transit. Yeah. You are. Why did you know how-- [LAUGH] a transit came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go right after Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go direct to Brunnlitz. Over there-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. I got to get Edith. She's going to tell you about this. I've got to tell you. Is I wouldn't separate from her. Is what she's doing in Plaszow, we know it's a transport. And she know where I go, and she liked to go. How is she go just the men? Crazy. You know? She dressed herself a man. And she got into this transport. Somehow you get killed, you get beaten. You've got-- somehow, you don't ask question. How she got-- she got in in this transport. The trouble was after a while, I don't know, this woman transport was canceled. And she went back to [INAUDIBLE]. And she couldn't go with us. All of the women from the Schindler's list, he brought them from Auschwitz. [INAUDIBLE]. He brought them from Plaszow to-- [INAUDIBLE] to Auschwitz. From Auschwitz, he brought them to Berlitz. They brought the-- the men brought the women. And Schindler brought them, [INAUDIBLE] the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to Brunnlitz. Did Edith get to Brunnlitz too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She couldn't go because the transport-- they canceled the transport. It was off. The reason, don't ask me. I don't know why. They got off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, d--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is she went back to [? her ?] barrack before, the women's barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow. And later, I don't know what was happened. It was a shame. I didn't know until she [INAUDIBLE] over there. She went to Auschwitz. From Auschwitz, she went to Ravensbruck. She went from Ravensbruck. She can tell you the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: She'll tell us. But now tell us what it was like at Brunnlitz for you. When was this when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES] Uh, when this was? If he was around-- and the whole war was all together, it was five, six months [INAUDIBLE]. Just like I said, in January-- In January, we was in transit. I was only a few days. It was horrible. They put us like sardines. They-- what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that 1944 by this time, or was it '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHS] No, '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was-- we was in '44 already in Gross-Rosen. In Gross-Rosen, he brought us for to [? Brenensi, ?] to Brunnlitz. Brunnlitz, we'd been only-- let's say from January, let's say even a short time in [INAUDIBLE] in Gross-Rosen, uh, maybe five months, six [? months until May. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Until the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So this was 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In January.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What was it like at Brunnlitz? What did you do? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGH] In Brunnlitz, make believe. He's so drastic. You saw the movie. It was terrible. For me, you must believe it's a concentration camp. You must believe. He, himself once in a while, one in every week [INAUDIBLE] they used to come to check on Schindler himself-- what he's do and what he produced. They come through, SS. He bribed them to give him money. And afterward, he was vulgar. He say in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. You know what this mean. He said to sh-- shit. Don't care. He, himself, after-- he'd been very, uh, personal with this people, because he got over there the 400, 500 from Krakow. That's what he lived to get [INAUDIBLE] in Krakow. For they worked for them in the, um, manufacturer for pots and pens. Let me finish this. I got enough. This is Schindler comes. Schindler was-- there's a lot of story in this. It-- it's make believe.
